[[co:commons:Category:Mirrors with animals and grapes design]][[ja:海獣葡萄鏡]][[zh:海獸葡萄鏡]] [[co:commons:Category:Mirrors with animals and grapes design]][[ja:海獣葡萄鏡]][[zh:海獸葡萄鏡]] [[ja:海獣葡萄鏡]][[zh:海獸葡萄鏡]] [[File:Chinese Bronze Mirror, Tang (29435463684).jpg|330px|thumb|Kaiyū Budō-kyō
The central figure of a crouching Sēngmí raising its head is identified as the "sea creature."]] '''Kaiyū Budō-kyō''' (海獣葡萄鏡) is a type of bronze mirror produced during the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}. The name derives from the patterns of grapevine arabesques and animal motifs on the mirror's reverse. However, the "sea creature" depicted is not a marine mammal but rather a mythical creature. These mirrors are considered emblematic of Tang-era mirrors and were imported to Japan during the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} and {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, where they were preserved in places like the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsō-in|1=Shōsōin}} and the {{ill|Hōryū-ji|en|Hōryū-ji|WD=Q261932|lt=Hōryū-ji|1=Hōryū-ji}} pagoda. Some notable burial and excavation finds, such as those from the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|en|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|WD=Q1039675|lt=Takamatsuzuka Kofun|1=Takamatsuzuka Tomb}}, have increased their prominence. Japan also produced {{ill|imitation mirrors|ja|仿製鏡|1=Bōseikyō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/imitation mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōseikyō|qq=}} domestically. In China, these mirrors are more commonly referred to as '''Ruishō Budō-kyō''' (瑞獣葡萄鏡).{{sfn|Ishiwata|2000|p=26-29}} == Overview == Kaiyū Budō-kyō were mass-produced during the Tang dynasty.{{note|Some scholars suggest the mirrors originated in the late {{ill|Sui dynasty|en|Sui dynasty|WD=Q7405|lt=Sui|1=Sui dynasty}} period.{{sfn|Katsube|1996|p=35-41}}{{sfn|Kotobank: Kaiyū Budō-kyō}}}} These thick bronze mirrors are decorated on the reverse with intricate grapevine patterns interspersed with various mythical and real creatures, such as {{ill|Sēngmí|ja|狻猊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sēngmí|1=Suanni|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q757972}}, [[:en:dragons|dragons]], {{ill|Chinese guardian lions|en|Chinese guardian lions|WD=Q1069772|lt=karajishi|1=Chinese guardian lions}}, {{ill|Qilin|en|Qilin|WD=Q14138|lt=qilin|1=Qilin}}, peacocks, {{ill|Makara (mythology)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makara (mythology)|qq=}}, birds, butterflies, bees, and dragonflies. The central knob, used for threading cords, often takes the shape of a beast, typically identified as a Sēngmí.{{sfn|Katsube|1996|p=28-31|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Ishiwata|2000|p=164-173}} The name "Kaiyū Budō-kyō" comes from the Qing-era catalog {{ill|Xiqing Gujian|zh|西清古鑑|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiqing Gujian|qq=}}, where the "sea creature" refers to the four-legged beast (Sēngmí) in the central motif. The exact reason for calling it a "sea creature" remains debated, with no consensus among scholars.{{sfn|Ishiwata|2000|p=18-19}}{{sfn|Katsube|1996|p=25-26}} The grapevine arabesque motif has its origins in the paradisiacal imagery associated with {{ill|Dionysus|en|Dionysus|WD=Q41680|lt=Dionysus|1=Dionysus}} and was transmitted to China via the {{ill|Silk Road|en|Silk Road|WD=Q36288|lt=Silk Road|1=Silk Road}}. It first appeared in Chinese textiles during the {{ill|Han dynasty|en|Han dynasty|WD=Q7209|lt=Han dynasty|1=Han dynasty}} and later became prominent in religious and decorative art during the [[:en:Northern Wei dynasty|Northern Wei dynasty]].{{sfn|Ishiwata|2000|p=98-104}} In Japan, these mirrors have been discovered in significant archaeological contexts, including tomb offerings, temple deposits, and ritual sites. Notable examples include items housed at {{ill|Shōsō-in|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōsō-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsuzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.{{sfn|Katsube|1996|p=275-277|qq=draft}} == Main Characteristics == [[File:海獣葡萄鏡の部分名称.svg|thumb|350px|Anatomy of Kaiyū Budō-kyō
Collection of the Kyoto National Museum.]] ;Knob (鈕) The central knob, used for threading cords, is often in the shape of a reclining mythical beast, typically a Sēngmí. Many knobs depict the beast raising its head, with the cord hole located beneath its body.{{sfn|Katsube|1996|p=64-70}} ;Inner and Outer Zones (内区・外区) The reverse is divided into an inner and outer zone, both adorned with grapevine patterns and various animals. The outer zone often includes birds and insects interspersed with the vines.{{sfn|Katsube|1996|p=70-71}} ;Decoration Bands (帯圏・外縁帯) A band encircles the edge of the mirror and may feature motifs such as palmette patterns, pomegranate designs, or serrated lines.{{sfn|Katsube|1996|p=71-72}} == Key Findings == * {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsō-in|1=Shōsōin}} treasures include multiple examples of Kaiyū Budō-kyō, such as the South Warehouse 7th and 8th mirrors. * {{ill|Hōryū-ji|en|Hōryū-ji|WD=Q261932|lt=Hōryū-ji|1=Hōryū-ji}} preserved mirrors include examples associated with the {{ill|reconstruction controversy|ja|法隆寺再建非再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/reconstruction controversy|1=Q125487168|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125487168}}. * The {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|en|Takamatsuzuka Tomb|WD=Q1039675|lt=Takamatsuzuka Kofun|1=Takamatsuzuka Tomb}} excavation revealed a mirror dated to the late 7th century.{{sfn|Katsube|1996|p=137-140}} == Notable Collections == * [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]]: The {{ill|Kaiyū Budō-kyō|ja|海獣葡萄鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaiyū Budō-kyō|1=Sea-beast grape mirror|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11151137}} held at {{ill|Katori Shrine|en|Katori Shrine|WD=Q372380|lt=Katori Shrine|1=Katori Jingū}} and {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|en|Ōyamazumi Shrine|WD=Q703904|lt=Ōyamazumi Shrine|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}}. * {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}: Mirrors excavated from {{ill|Takamatsuzuka Kofun|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamatsuzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Hōryū-ji|ja|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}}. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=石渡美江 |title=楽園の図像-海獣葡萄鏡の誕生 |publisher=吉川弘文館 |year=2000 |isbn=4-642-05497-9}} * {{Cite book|author=勝部明生 |title=海獣葡萄鏡の研究 |publisher=臨川書店 |year=1996
